title: Citrus chiffon cake
subtitle: American oil-based sponge with orange and lemon, light and moist
excerpt: A chiffon cake bridges the lightness of a meringue-risen sponge with the rich moisture of an oil-based batter. The yolks are beaten with oil, citrus juice, and flour; the whites are whisked separately to stiff peaks and folded in. Like angel food cake, chiffon is baked in an ungreased tube tin and cooled inverted. The result is taller, moister, and more tender than a génoise and does not dry out as quickly.
sourceNotes: The chiffon cake was invented by Harry Baker, a California insurance salesman, in the 1920s and kept secret for two decades. He sold the recipe to General Mills in 1947, which published it in Betty Crocker's promotional materials that same year. The recipe entered the public domain through this widely distributed publication; it was reprinted in countless American home-baking titles from 1948 onwards, including the 1950 and 1956 editions of Better Homes and Gardens Cook Book (widely circulated, not currently commercially active).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake a day ahead and store inverted in the tin at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 140°C. The low temperature allows the tall, oil-rich batter to bake evenly to the centre without a set exterior trapping a raw middle. Bake for 55 to 60 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the surface springs back when pressed.

glossaryTerms.definition: The step in which stiffly beaten egg whites (a meringue) are folded into the heavier oil-and-yolk batter to provide the leavening for a chiffon cake. The meringue must be firm enough to hold its structure but not so stiff it forms dry clumps that won't incorporate. The technique is to add a large spoonful of meringue to the batter and stir it in to lighten the mixture first, then fold in the remaining meringue in two batches using a large metal spoon. The aim is to retain as many air bubbles as possible while achieving a homogeneous batter with no white streaks.

content.text: The chiffon method combines a yolk-and-oil batter with whisked egg whites. The critical skill is
folding the meringue into the batter
without deflating the whites. As with angel food cake, the tin must not be greased, and the cake must cool inverted in the tin.

attrs.prepNote: divided: 150g for the yolk batter, 50g for the meringue

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C conventional (140°C fan). Do not grease the tin. Sift the flour, 150g of the sugar, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. Make a well and add the oil, egg yolks, orange juice, both zests, and vanilla. Whisk until smooth and slightly glossy.

In a clean stand mixer bowl, whisk the egg whites and cream of tartar to soft peaks. Gradually add the remaining 50g sugar and whisk to firm, glossy peaks. Stir a large spoonful of the meringue into the yolk batter to loosen it. Pour the loosened batter over the remaining meringue and fold together gently with a large metal spoon until just combined with no white streaks.

Pour into the ungreased tin. Bake for 55 to 60 minutes until golden on top, a skewer comes out clean, and the surface springs back when pressed. Invert immediately onto a rack (or over a bottle). Cool completely inverted, at least 1 hour. Run a knife around the edges and unmould. Dust with icing sugar to serve, or glaze with a simple orange juice and icing sugar drizzle.
